DECLARATION OF PAMELA WHITEHEAD IN SUPPORT OF DEFENDANTS JOHN KEVLIN AND REUBEN JUIUS AND ROSE LLPS STRATEGIC LAWSUIT AGAINST PUBLIC PARTICIPATION (TRANSACTION ID # 58731228) FILED BY DEFENDANT KEVLIN, JOHN REUBEN JUNIUS & ROSE, LLP

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANTS JOHN KEVLIN, AND REUBEN, JUNIUS & ROSE, LLP'S SPECIAL MOTION TO STRIKE STRATEGIC LAWSUIT AGAINST PUBLIC PARTICIPATION IS GRANTED AND MR. KEVLIN AND HIS LAW FIRM ARE AWARDED ATTORNEY'S FEES OF $13,851. THE COURT STRIKES ALL CLAIMS AGAINST DEFENDANTS JOHN KEVLIN, AND REUBEN, JUNIUS & ROSE, LLP. FIRST, THE COURT FINDS DEFENDANTS HAVE SATISFIED THE FIRST PRONG TO SHOW THAT ALL CLAIMS ALLEGED AGAINST THEM ARE BASED ON PROTECTED ACTIVITY BECAUSE THE ACTIVITY OCCURED IN CONNECTION WITH THE DRAFTING OF AN AGREEMENT TO SETTLE A DISPUTE. THE SELZER CASE PROVIDES THE LEGAL SUPPORT FOR THE COURT'S FINDING. SECOND, THE COURT FINDS PLAINTIFF HAS NOT SATISFIED HIS BURDEN AS TO THE SECOND PRONG TO SHOWN MINIMAL MERIT. THE CLAIMS DO NOT STATE COGNIZABLE CLAIMS PURSUANT TO CALIFORNIA LAW. EVEN IF THE CLAIMS DID STATE COGNIZABLE CLAIMS, THE COURT FINDS NO POSSIBLE DAMAGES AT LEAST UNLESS AND UNTIL THERE HAS BEEN A DETERMINATION THAT THE "POISON PILL" LANGUAGE BARRED THE PLAINTIFF FROM ENFORCING THE SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT AS PLAINTIFF BELIEVES THE SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT WAS INTENDED. AS NO SUCH RULING HAS BEEN MADE, ANY SUCH DAMAGES (WHICH COULD ONLY OCCUR IN THE FUTURE) HAVE NOT OCCURED YET, AND SO THERE ARE NO DAMAGES NOW ON WHICH TO SUE ON. REGARDING THE AWARD OF ATTORNEY'S FEES, THE COURT ONLY AWARDS NOW BASED OFF WHAT HAS BEEN ASKED BY DEFENDANTS SO FAR. IF DEFENDANTS WANT TO REQUEST MORE, DEFENDANTS MUST MAKE A SEPARATE MOTION. AS TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUEST FOR RULING ON OBJECTIONS, THE COURT DID NOT CONSIDER ANY EVIDENCE THAT WAS RAISED FOR THE FIRST TIME IN DEFENDANTS' REPLY MEMO. PREVAILING PARTY SHALL PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER TO THE COURT FOR SIGNATURE WHICH MAY BE E-MAIL TO CONTESTDEPT302TR@SFTC.ORG. APPEARANCES - ATTY. STEPHEN M. WILLIAM, TEL. NO. 1-415-292-3656 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
NO OTHER APPEARANCES ON THE MATTER.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendants John Kevlin and Reuben Junius & Rose's motion for recovery of additional attorney's fees is denied in large part. Mr. Kevlin and the Reuben firm are awarded additional fees of $3,000. While the court does not doubt that counsel for the moving parties expended the time they say they spent in filing reply papers and preparing for and attending oral argument on their anti-SLAPP motion, the fees they seek are far out of proportion to the issues raised by the opposition papers and are therefore were not reasonably incurred for purposes of the fee recovery provisions of the anti-SLAPP statute. Defendants were entitled to have their attorneys do extremely thorough work, but that does not make the fees incurred in doing that work compensable. This was a fairly straight-forward anti-SLAPP motion. Defendants have already been awarded significant fees, including a rough estimate of the fees they expected to incur for their reply papers and the hearing. A review of the record on the anti-SLAPP motion and the court's recollection of the hearing persuades the court that additional fees of $3,000 should be awarded, but no more. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR # 5976 =302/hk

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Stay Order Confirming That An Automatic Stay Is In Force During The Pendency Of Plaintiffs Appeal
Off Calendar Jun-16-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Stephen Williams of Law Offices of Stephen M. Williams telephone number 415-292-3656 for Plaintiff Irving Zaretsky.
Anthony Giles of Law Office of Anthony Giles telephone number 415-839-2099 for Defendant Pamela Whitehead.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF IRVING ZARETSKY'S Motion For Stay Order Confirming That An Automatic Stay Is In Force During The Pendency Of Plaintiffs Appeal is denied. The motion lacks merit on both procedural and substantive grounds. The motion is procedurally deficient because there is no statute, case or other authority recognizing a motion such as this one. The premise of the motion is that Mr. Zaretsky's appeal automatically (i.e, without need for any further act) stays (i.e. defers the effect of) the May 20, 2016 order. If that is true, then there is no need for this court to say so. If the premise of this motion is correct, then regardless of how I rule on the motion, the parties' rights and obligations are unaffected. Notably the motion does not seek any coercive relief, but only my views as to whether an automatic stay is in effect. If this motion were a request for a stay of the May 20 order pending appeal (which is a recognized motion), I would exercise my discretion to deny the motion for the same reasons I granted defendants' motion which led to the May 20 order. But this motion asserts that I have no discretion and the real relief that Mr. Zaretsky seeks already occurred at the time he filed his appeal and asks me to confirm that. Even apart from the lack of any authority for such a confirmation motion, even if I were to grant the motion, I fail to see how doing so has any effect on anyone. If there is an automatic stay in effect and I confirm it, I have rendered a (correct) advisory opinion. If there is no automatic stay in effect and I nonetheless confirm that there is one, I have made a (wrong) advisory opinion. If there is an automatic stay in effect and I refuse to confirm it, I have made a (wrong) advisory opinion. If there is no automatic stay in effect and I refuse to confirm that there is a stay, I have rendered a (correct) advisory opinion. In all four instances, all that I would be doing is rendering an advisory opinion. The motion is substantively deficient because the May 20 order is not a mandatory injunction. In substance, that order was simply a contemplated second phase of the initial motion for a prohibitory injunction brought by Mr. Zaretsky which was deferred until defendants chose to file a motion seeking a determination whether my initial ruling did or did not prohibit them from building the roof they wished to build. Accordingly, in my view, it is error to characterize the May 20 order as modifying the initial preliminary injunction order since its purpose and effect was to determine what is and is not allowable under the initial preliminary injunction order. I believe it is in derogation of the CCP 916 stay rules and the purposes of those rules for the appeal of the May 20 order to effect an automatic stay because the order, for all practical purposes, only served to reflect my view that there is no legal impediment for the defendants to build the roof that they wish to build. At no time have I required or mandated the defendants to build or not build anything. All that I have done is delineated and refined the scope of what they are prohibited from doing. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)

Off Calendar Mar-23-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. STEPHEN M. WILLIAMS, ESQ. of Law Offices of Stephen M. Williams, Tel No. 415-292-3656 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Attys. STUART M. GORDON, ESQ. and JAMES K. HOLDER, ESQ. of Gordon & Rees LLP, Tel No. 415-986-5900 appearing for Defendant John Kevlin and Reuben, Junius & Rose, LLP;

The Court provides parties with a corrected tentative ruling. Plaintiff elects to proceed today with hearing over the Court's offer to continue the matter if Plaintiff believes he has been prejudiced. The Court grants leave to permit this motion to be heard after the sixty day timeframe, and for the motion to be heard on the merits today. Matter argued and submitted by counsel. DEFENDANTS JOHN KEVLIN, AND REUBEN, JUNIUS & ROSE, LLP'S Special Motion To Strike Strategic Lawsuit Against Public Participation is granted and Mr. Kevlin and his law firm are awarded attorney's fees of $13,851. The Court strikes all claims against defendants John Kevlin, and Reuben, Junius & Rose, LLP. First, the Court finds defendants have satisfied the first prong to show that all claims alleged against them are based on protected activity because the activity occured in connection with the drafting of an agreement to settle a dispute. The Selzer case provides the legal support for the Court's finding. Second, the Court finds Plaintiff has not satisfied his burden as to the second prong to shown minimal merit. The claims do not state cognizable claims pursuant to California law. Even if the claims did state cognizable claims, the Court finds no possible damages at least unless and until there has been a determination that the "poison pill" language barred the Plaintiff from enforcing the settlement agreement as Plaintiff believes the settlement agreement was intended. As no such ruling has been made, any such damages (which could only occur in the future) have not occured yet, and so there are no damages now on which to sue on. Regarding the award of attorney's fees, the Court only awards now based off what has been asked by defendants so far. If defendants want to request more, defendants must make a separate motion. As to Plaintiff's request for ruling on objections, the Court did not consider any evidence that was raised for the first time in Defendants' reply memo. Prevailing party shall prepare and submit a proposed order to the Court for signature which may be e-mail to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.
